nativeRange.habitat: Lake Inle and its immediately connected waters in Shan State, Myanmar — a large, shallow calcareous highland lake with clear, hard, alkaline water over silty and sandy substrate. Dense emergent and submerged aquatic vegetation, including Vallisneria, Hydrilla, and Ceratophyllum, is characteristic of its natural habitat.

decorPreferences: dense planting with fine-leaved plants (Vallisneria, Java moss, hornwort, Egeria)
open midwater swimming lanes for shoaling behaviour
dark substrate to enhance iridescent colouration
gentle flow — low to moderate surface agitation
floating plants or emergent vegetation to diffuse light and reduce skittishness

diet.notes: In the wild, the Emerald Dwarf Rasbora feeds on small invertebrates, zooplankton, and algae. In the aquarium it generally accepts high-quality micro-pellets, finely crushed flakes, and frozen or live micro-foods such as baby brine shrimp nauplii, micro worms, cyclops, daphnia, and Moina. Because the mouth is very small, food particle size is critical — standard flakes should be crushed to a fine powder before feeding. Offering a variety of foods helps maintain colouration and condition.

goodWith: other small peaceful Danionidae and microrasboras
Sawbwa resplendens (Asian Rummy-Nose Rasbora) — shared Lake Inle biotope and water parameters
small hillstream loaches tolerant of hard alkaline water
dwarf shrimp (adults generally safe; fry may be picked off)
nano corydoras in matching water parameters
avoidWith: large or boisterous fish that outcompete for food
nippy species (tiger barbs, serpae tetras, some barbs)
fish requiring soft, acidic water (incompatible chemistry)
fast-moving or substantially larger fish that stress timid shoals

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Danio erythromicron; check FishBase image page for exact photographer attribution before use.
Wikimedia Commons image filed under legacy name Microrasbora erythromicron; verify current license and attribution on commons page before use.

summary: The Emerald Dwarf Rasbora (Danio erythromicron) is a tiny Lake Inle endemic reaching only around 2 cm, with iridescent blue-green body bars and vivid orange-red fins. Despite its trade name, it is not a true rasbora and belongs to genus Danio. Like its lake-mate Sawbwa resplendens, it requires hard, alkaline water (pH 7.5–8.5). IUCN Endangered.
careNotes:
The Emerald Dwarf Rasbora was formerly sold under the name Microrasbora erythromicron, a placement that reflected its small size and superficial resemblance to rasboras rather than true phylogenetic affinity. Molecular and morphological research transferred it to genus Danio (family Danionidae), where it now sits alongside the Celestial Pearl Danio (Danio margaritatus) — a more popular Lake Inle endemic that can be confused with it in the trade. The older name Microrasbora erythromicron is still commonly encountered in hobby literature.

The most important husbandry consideration is water chemistry. Lake Inle is a shallow calcareous highland lake at roughly 900 m elevation in Shan State, Myanmar, with naturally hard, alkaline, clear water produced by the surrounding limestone geology. The Emerald Dwarf Rasbora is adapted to pH 7.5–8.5 and moderate to high hardness (gH 10–25). Keeping this species in the soft, acidic water typically recommended for small Southeast Asian cyprinids tends to lead to rapid decline — the same caveat applies to its lake-mate Sawbwa resplendens. Mineral supplementation may be required in soft-water areas; crushed coral or limestone substrate helps buffer pH upward.

At a maximum of around 2 cm total length, the Emerald Dwarf Rasbora is among the smallest cyprinids available in the hobby — noticeably smaller than the Celestial Pearl Danio (around 2.5 cm) and considerably smaller than most rasboras. Its timid, retiring character means it generally fares poorly against boisterous or fast-moving tankmates; nano biotope setups with Sawbwa resplendens or hillstream loaches sharing the same calcareous-lake chemistry are frequently recommended. Groups of at least 8 — ideally 12 or more — are needed for natural shoaling behaviour and to prevent stress-induced hiding. Dense fine-leaved planting is essential for confidence.

Temperature should remain in the cooler range (20–26°C); sustained temperatures above 27°C are generally poorly tolerated. The species is listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List, driven by agricultural eutrophication of Lake Inle, invasive species introductions (particularly introduced cyprinids), and collection pressure. Captive-bred specimens are strongly preferred when available.
breedingNotes: The Emerald Dwarf Rasbora is an egg-scatterer, and breeding is achievable in a dedicated species or breeding tank but is generally considered moderately difficult. Conditioning a small group or pair on varied live and frozen micro-foods over several weeks helps bring fish into spawning condition. Spawning typically occurs among fine-leaved plants or Java moss, with adhesive eggs scattered among the plant leaves. Adults will consume eggs, so a spawning mop or dense moss clump should be moved to a separate rearing vessel promptly after spawning, or a mesh divider used to protect the eggs. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 24°C. Fry are very small and require infusoria or commercially prepared first-fry foods for the first one to two weeks before they are large enough to accept baby brine shrimp nauplii. Sexing is straightforward in mature fish: males display more intense blue-green iridescence and deeper orange-red fin pigmentation, while females are noticeably deeper-bodied when gravid.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Danio erythromicron
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes; reclassified from Microrasbora by Conway et al. 2009), native range (Lake Inle, Myanmar), water parameters (pH 7.0–8.0, hardness 90–268 ppm / ~5–15 dH, temperature 20–26°C), adult size (~2 cm TL), diet, breeding notes, and IUCN Endangered status. Hard alkaline water requirement confirmed; soft-acid water contraindicated.

additional.name: FishBase — Danio erythromicron
additional.notes: Cross-referenced adult size (max 2.0 cm TL), taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes), native range (Inle Lake, Myanmar), depth range, diet, and IUCN status (Endangered). Confirmed synonymy with Microrasbora erythromicron.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Emerald Dwarf Rasbora Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide providing husbandry parameters, minimum group size, tank size recommendation, diet (micro-pellets, baby brine shrimp), compatible tankmates, and hard-water chemistry requirement for the hobby context.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Danio erythromicron species profile
additional.notes: Species profile confirming Lake Inle endemic status, Danio reclassification, hard alkaline water requirement, small adult size, timid shoaling behaviour, and Endangered conservation status.

additional.name: Conway K.W. et al. (2009) — Phylogenetic placement of Microrasbora
additional.notes: Conway, Mayden & Tang (2009) 'Phylogenetic relationships of Danio within the order Cypriniformes' — the peer-reviewed analysis that transferred Microrasbora erythromicron to Danio. Cited as basis for current accepted taxonomy.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning is achievable in a species tank furnished with fine-leaved plants or a Java moss spawning mop, but adults will consume eggs — the spawning mop or parents should be moved promptly after spawning. Fry are very small and require infusoria or commercially prepared first-fry food for the first one to two weeks. Sexing is straightforward in mature fish: males show more intense colouration than females.
